video_title: TEKNOLOGI SEMIKONDUKTOR | Malaysia Bangunkan Cip AI Bagi Pasaran Dunia - Anwar
comment:
Kecerdasan Buatan (Artificial intelligence - AI)
Mungkin boleh digunakan untuk membenteras elemen rasuah dan salahguna kuasa dalam sektor perkhidmatan awam?

Apabila seseorang penjawat awam mempunyai gaya hidup mewah yang tidak berpadanan dengan gaji pendapatan bulanan sebagai penjawat awam,

AI dengan program tertentu boleh membuat semak silang data terutamanya data² pemilikan aset dan pemilikan akaun perbankan yang berkaitan dengan aliran kewangan individu dan syarikat?

Data² pemilikan seperti :
✅ Pemilikan kenderaan (jumlah yang luar biasa dan berharga mewah), melibatkan pelbagai agensi milik kerajaan seperti JPJ dan rekod pelupusan pemilikan kenderaan (mencairkan aset kepada bentuk wang tunai)
✅ Pemilikan Hartanah pada jumlah luar biasa dan harga diluar kemampuan seorang penjawat awam, melibatkan beberapa agensi dan jabatan kerajaan di peringkat persekutuan dan negeri (pihak berkuasa tempatan seperti rekod cukai pintu sebagai pemilik hartanah dan pejabat tanah iaitu rekod cukai tanah sebagai pemilik hartanah jenis bertanah atau berstrata)
dan rekod pelupusan pemilikan hartanah (mencairkan aset kepada bentuk wang tunai)
✅ Pemilikan akaun bank, pemilikan kad kredit premium dengan tahap had perbelanjaan yang luar biasa, rekod loan serta pembayaran loan dan akaun E-wallet, yang melibatkan BNM dan beberapa agensi berkaitan 
✅ SSM, bagi mereka dalam perkhidmatan awam yang mempunyai pendapatan perniagaan...dengan akaun syarikat beraudit dan kesahihan keuntungan pendapatan perniagaan dan berkait dengan data di LHDN
✅ LHDN, data² rekod pembelian pemilikan harta alih atau harta tidak alih yang dibeli secara tunai atau dipindahmilik secara tempoh yang pendek diluar kebiasaan sesuatu pemilikan harta alih dan harta tidak alih sebagai seorang penjawat awam
✅ Rekod KASTAM, bagi pelbagai pembayaran berkaitan import export barangan jika berkaitan pendapatan perniagaan yang disahkan oleh SSM
✅ Rekod caruman KWSP (cerminan pendapatan bulanan)
✅ Rekod PERKESO, jika terlibat sebagai majikan atau pekerja yang mencarum (cerminan kepada pendapatan yang diraihkan daripada pelbagai aktiviti perniagaan disahkan oleh SSM)
✅ Bursa Saham dan institusi pelaburan saham seperti ASB PNB Tabung Haji, berkaitan pemilikan saham pada jumlah yang luar biasa bagi kemampuan dengan pendapatan gaji bulanan seorang penjawat awam
✅ Rekod IMIGRESEN berkaitan kekerapan perjalanan ke luar negara (melancong ke destinasi yang memerlukan perbelanjaan besar) dengan kerap dalam setahun 
✅ Rekod-rekod Pembelian barangan jenama mewah (jam tangan, bag 🛍️ tangan, dan lain-lain) secara tunai atau cash atau sebaliknya 
✅ Rekod-rekod Pembelian logam berharga (seperti emas) dan barangan perhiasan mewah secara tunai atau sebaliknya (rantai leher bertatahkan berlian dan sebagainya)
✅Rekod pemilikan sijil-sijil insurans atau takaful (melibatkan pembayaran premium bulanan pada harga yang luar biasa)
✅ Paparan gaya hidup di semua platform media sosial juga merupakan suatu cerminan atau tanda-tanda awal

Amalan kebiasaan apabila terlibat dengan elemen rasuah dan salahguna kuasa, pastinya akan melibatkan ahli keluarga terdekat, pasangan (suami / isteri), anak-anak, ibubapa, saudara-mara atau proksi rakan-rakan yang mempunyai syarikat perniagaan (bertujuan untuk parking duit dengan menggunakan platform fasiliti akaun kewangan milik syarikat bagi pelbagai perbelanjaan dan pembayaran gaya hidup) untuk mengalihkan hasil yang diperolehi daripada aktiviti tersebut.

Mungkin manfaat teknologi AI ini boleh dilaksanakan untuk melakukan semak silang data dalam sektor perkhidmatan awam bagi elemen membenteras gejala dan budaya rasuah serta salahguna kuasa jawatan dalam perkhidmatan awam di Malaysia 🇲🇾
comment_en:
Artificial Intelligence (AI)

Could Artificial Intelligence (AI) be used to combat corruption and abuse of power in the public sector?

When a public servant has a luxurious lifestyle that is clearly inconsistent with their monthly salary, AI, through specialized programs, could perform cross-checks across multiple databases—especially records of asset ownership and banking information related to the financial activities of individuals and companies.

The data that could be cross-referenced includes:

✅ Vehicle ownership (an unusually large number of vehicles or ownership of luxury vehicles), involving multiple government agencies such as the Road Transport Department (JPJ), including records of vehicle sales or disposals (converting assets into cash).
✅ Property ownership involving an unusually high number of properties or properties beyond what a public servant could reasonably afford, using records from local authorities (property assessment tax), land offices (land tax and ownership records for landed and strata properties), and records of property sales or transfers (converting assets into cash).
✅ Bank accounts, premium credit cards with unusually high spending limits, loan records and repayments, and e-wallet accounts, involving the central bank (BNM) and other relevant agencies.
✅ Business ownership records (SSM) for public servants with business income, including audited company accounts, verification of business profits, and matching this information with tax records maintained by the Inland Revenue Board (LHDN).
✅ LHDN (tax authority) records, including purchases of movable and immovable assets made in cash or assets transferred within unusually short periods, where such ownership appears inconsistent with a public servant's income.
✅ Customs records, including import and export payments related to business income verified through SSM.
✅ Employees Provident Fund (EPF/KWSP) contribution records, which reflect monthly employment income.
✅ Social Security Organization (PERKESO) records, if the individual is an employer or employee making contributions, reflecting income from business activities verified by SSM.
✅ Stock market and investment institutions, such as Bursa Malaysia, ASB, PNB, and Tabung Haji, to identify unusually large investment holdings that do not match the person's salary.
✅ Immigration records, particularly frequent overseas travel to expensive destinations requiring significant spending.
✅ Records of luxury goods purchases, such as luxury watches, handbags, and other branded products, whether paid in cash or through other methods.
✅ Purchases of precious metals and luxury jewelry, such as gold, diamond necklaces, and similar items.
✅ Insurance or takaful policies, particularly those involving unusually high monthly premium payments.
✅ Lifestyle displays on social media platforms, which may serve as early indicators of possible corruption or abuse of power.

The author further argues that corruption and abuse of power often involve close family members, spouses, children, parents, relatives, or proxy associates who own businesses. These individuals or companies may allegedly be used to hold or move illicit funds through corporate financial accounts to finance lavish lifestyles.

Therefore, the author suggests that AI technology could be implemented to perform comprehensive cross-checks across government databases to help combat corruption and abuse of power within Malaysia's public service.

video_title: Malaysia Aims To Become AI Powerhouse In Southeast Asia | CNA Correspondent
comment:
Without the latest advanced AI chips from Nvidia, AMD. Dont expect us to become a leader of AI in South East Asia. Becoming a pioneer/ leader in AI, requires substantial resources: Funding, Highly-skilled workforce, Hardware, etc. We have have a funding, but can we really compete with the pioneers in the US that throw Billions of dollars for development?

 Those glorifying Malaysian semi conductor industry is laughable, Malaysia do no MANUFACTURE these chips in Malaysia (latest 3-5 nanometer chips), the semi conductor industry in Malaysia serve a crucial role in the global supply chain  (packaging, testing, and assembly). That doesn't mean Malaysia will be able to acquire these advanced chips to become the leader in AI for S.E.A. I love it when AI is thrown around and glorified. As of now, Malaysia's local fab only produces (historically 110nm to 40nm), currently 28nm to 22nm. We do not MANUFACTURE 3-5nm chips, we just provide them with assembly, testing and packaging. LOL,

comment:
Economic reforms by PM Anwar
With the approval of Johore sultan, Johore becomes an economic zone with the model city Johore Bahru in 2027.
Infrastructure revamping
1st phase infrastructure - RTS linking Bukit Chagar with Woodland station.
Will attract 200000Singapore to JB daily, to work, stay, and makan .
Another 200000 travel by causeways to JB.
2nd phase infrastructure - High speed rail , starts from Jurong East station, to JB station (connected via Islander Puteri LRT and Chagar station .) link to Hua Hin and Bangkok stations ( Thailand) joining to Vietaine ( ( Laos) and end at Kunming station .
Will attract one million rich China migrants to invest,  stay and study in JB.
Economic slumps in Singapore  to -1.5 % from 2027 onwards. Big and medium enterprises like Tech giants, F&B, semiconductor industries, education centres from Singapore will diversify their businesses to Johore .
On the contrary,2028 economics growth in Johore escalates to 15% yearly . Johore GDP per capita income enjoys a $45000 US annually making her the highest income state of S.E.Asia.
A law passes down to allow one country two system to be practiced in Malaysia in the year 2040.
State merger
There will be a state merger between Johore and Singapore in the year 2040.
Johore and Singapore compile their national reserves for further developments

@SaintAmazigh  
When you say talent leaving the country, you're talking of people who have already computed that from Day 1 of their IT-related studies.

These kids know their stuff and are adept in rapidly changing work environs.

video_title: Malaysia Aims To Become AI Powerhouse In Southeast Asia | CNA Correspondent
comment:
The timing of this report is just too perfect. On Monday, US tech stocks fell massively as investors began to wisen up to the fact that AI is an overhyped bubble.

The entire report omits the Achilles heel of AI: the massive cost of running these AI algorithms. People think that using AI to replace workers will save them money until they look at that massive compute bill.

Also, AI is extremely limited in the things it can do. AI is really good at recognizing and replicating patterns if there is human data collected for those patterns, but AI cannot effectively reason/make logical inferences or deductions. In other words, AI is not AI: there is no intelligence involved in these foundation models.

I find it funny that Malaysia is perpetually chasing the coat tails of Western countries. Not everything that the mat salleh is trying to sell you is gold. They may very well just be trying to pump up their stock prices only.
comment_en: The timing of this report is just too perfect. On Monday, US tech stocks fell massively as investors began to widen up to the fact that AI is an overhyped bubble. The entire report omits the Achilles heel of AI: the massive cost of running these AI algorithms. People think that using AI to replace workers will save them money until they look at that massive computing bill. Also, AI is extremely limited in the things it can do. AI is really good at recognizing and replicating patterns if there is human data collected for those patterns, but AI can't effectively reason/make logical inferences or deductions. In other words, AI is not AI: there is no intelligence involved in these foundation models. I find it funny that Malaysia is perpetually chasing the coat tails of Western countries. Not everything that the matleh is trying to sell you gold. They may just be trying to pump up their stock prices well.

comment:
@ExtrusionXDesigns  Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to systems that are designed to exceed the capabilities of the human brain, particularly in learning, reasoning, and discovering new information. Unlike traditional programming, where developers must write specific code for every possible scenario, AI systems—especially those utilizing machine learning, whether supervised or unsupervised—can adapt and improve over time.

A basic AI, like the "Mindy" chatbot from M1, is an example of a system with limited capabilities. It’s programmed to respond to specific keywords and lacks the ability to understand or address complex issues that a consumer might explain. These types of AI are not truly intelligent; they are more akin to traditional computer programs that have existed for decades and offer minimal innovation in problem-solving or learning

A basic AI, like the "Mindy" chatbot from M1, is an example of a system with limited capabilities. comment: Saying Penang is “just cheap labour” is outdated,that term have existed since the 90s and South Korea,Taiwan was all given that label. If companies actually want cheap labour they would have gone to India or Vietnam or even Indonesia. The reality is that Malaysia sits in the middle of the value chain in semiconductor and we are actually making leeway climbing up that value chain. I actually don't understand the obsession with wafer fabs. That's red ocean strategy. Optics and Advanced packaging is where the next big opportunity lies, because bleeding edge silicon is hitting the limits of physics. Even TSMC is expanding downstream, because that's where the next big opportunity lies.  Furthermore Vitrox growth is actually very impressive for such a young company and they are expecting to reach RM 1 billion in revenue this year.From what I know Vitrox actually design and develop their own machine vision systems,inspection algorithms,using proprietary systems they design internally and not just reselling/relabel Chinese products.
comment_en: Saying Penang is "just cheap labor" is outdated,that term has existed since the 90s and South Korea,Taiwan was all given that label.If companies really want cheap labor they would have gone to India or Vietnam or even Indonesia.The reality is that Malaysia sits in the middle of the value chain in semiconductor and we are actually making room to climb up that value chain.I actually don't understand the obsession with wafer fabs.That's the red ocean strategy.Optics and Advanced packaging is where the next big opportunity lies,because bleeding edge silicon is hitting the limits of physics.Even TSMC is expanding downstream,because that's where the big opportunity lies.Moreover Vitrox growth is actually very impressive for such a young company and they are expecting to reach RM1 billion in revenue this year.From what I know Vitrolabs and their machine vision systems, they actually develop their own proprietary design/re-selling systems, not just internal products.

video_title: Malaysia launches National Artificial Intelligence Office
comment:
Automated Citizen Support:
• Deploy AI-powered chatbots and virtual assistants on government websites and mobile apps to handle routine inquiries, provide real-time assistance, and process simple service requests 24/7.

Streamlined Administrative Processes:
• Use AI algorithms for data entry, document verification, and form processing to minimize manual labor and reduce processing times.
• Implement natural language processing (NLP) to automatically read and extract relevant information from applications and reports.

Predictive Analytics for Resource Management:
• Leverage machine learning models to forecast demand for various services, optimize budget allocation, and predict maintenance needs in public infrastructure.

Fraud Detection and Compliance:
• Employ AI tools to monitor transactions, flag suspicious activities, and ensure compliance with regulations, reducing losses due to fraud and administrative errors.

Digitalization of Public Records:
• Automate the digitization and indexing of records to improve retrieval speeds and support data-driven decision-making across departments.

Efficient Workflow Integration:
• Integrate AI systems into interdepartmental workflows, ensuring seamless communication and reducing bureaucratic delays by routing tasks automatically.    

Put everything in place before our neighbor, Singapore, does it first. We would be the most efficient government in the world.

video_title: Will AI Take Over Our Jobs in Malaysia?
comment:
Will A.I take over our jobs?.  It all depends which industries you are talking about e.g. Some restaurants are already putting robotics or A.I belt conveyor deliveries to customer.  Warehouse management system that utilized robotic system to pick accurately from specified locations.  Currently, in Singapore the MRT trains are fully automated and they are talking about buses and taxis next in line.  Some manufacturing assembly lines in China are fully automated working 24 x 7 days.

For Artificial Intelligence to really work in real time situation, you really need 5G > good network coverage to monitor the system put in place.  In Malaysia, the network infrastructure is still behind Singapore and large investment needed to cover the whole places.  Human intervention is still preferred even though we can automate the whole process.  A.I. can  help to relieve some of the more arduous repetitive tasks while the human still monitor the situation.

Currently, A.I will definitely help some people in the area of creativeness, consultancy, teaching tools , etc.  You have to be specific in defining the right parameters feeding the A.I. system.  If you asked the wrong thing, the A.I will still give you the solutions but its not the answer to your problem.

Lastly, will A.I completely take over the whole human process in today society and workplace?  I don't think so without the 5G infrastructure and governing safety laws that navigate the system.  The legal process will take time to implement and in case of accidents involved with A.I. who will be responsible for it.
comment_en: It all depends on which industries you are talking about e.g. Some restaurants are already putting robotics or A.I belt conveyor deliveries to customer. Warehouse management system that utilized robotic system to pick accurately from specified locations. Currently, in Singapore the MRT trains are fully automated and they are talking about buses and taxis next in line. Some manufacturing assembly lines in China are fully automated working 24 x 7 days. For Artificial Intelligence to really work in real time situation, you really need 5G > good network coverage to monitor the system put in place. In Malaysia, the network infrastructure is still behind Singapore and large investment needed to cover the whole places. Human intervention is still preferred even though we can automate the whole process. A.I. can help to relieve the more arduous tasks of the human infrastructure, and they are talking about buses and taxis next in line. If you still have the right to implement the A.I. system, you will definitely take some time to implement the right solutions and provide the right people in the right area without consulting the legal parameters and the right people in the wrong area.

video_title: Will AI Take Over Our Jobs in Malaysia?
comment:
1. Too general. We can't just cluster all the consumer grade AI like free ChatGPT, Gemini, Tesla AutoPilot. There are small language model that can we can be made that runs much cheaper much more reliable and "better" based on all the definitions given.
2. Accountability. Depends on who the "programmer" is. If you are using free ChatGPT, YOU the consumer are the "programmer". The role PROMPT ENGINEER exists for this (it's no longer the highest demand job in the space btw). Google and Claude have released manuals for this. However in the case where your Tesla AutoPilot crashes, then Tesla is responsible as you are not programming it. If you decide to run OpenPilot to run on your Corolla, then again YOU are the programmer. Same thing for who the "WE" is. If an organization makes their own small language models for the roles they require, then you have full control. Built on top of open source models.
3. Accuracy. Models coupled with given it tools like web search can give you citation and reason. Good example would be Perplexity with the Sonar model.
4. How do we adapt?  Find a role, example an accountant. Use a large language model, learn some basic prompt engineering and see what tasks you can automate. The stuff the LLM can do, upskill on the stuff it cannot. Decision accountability should be on the human.
5. "AI cannot create original content", the Nobel Prize for Chemistry went to Dennis Hassabis who leads Google Deepmind. He is the ultimate AI dev, with no strong background in Chemistry. Pattern recognition and action is something LLMs or SLM are good at. Run this experiment:
"Generate a haiku about personal finance using only words that start with W". You will get an a haiku never before written.
6. On productivity. Before Excel, lets say you want to schedule classes your students in school, you need a programmer to write the algorithm to write the software to handle this complexity. That was Bill Gates first job btw. Now Excel proficiency is required for any office position and most say they do.
7. On the digital gap. Digital gaps have always existed since we were able to make money on the internet in the 90s. How did we deal with that? Introduce at the school level. We started off by replacing the z-distribution booklet to using scientific calculators, and then we allowed scientific calculators for purposes beyond that one Probability and Stat chapters. The syllabus has to change.
8. Ong number, last one. I disagree that using AI is easier than learning how to use Whatsapp or e-commerce. This is because consumer grade LLMs are very dangerous to use correctly. "It's always cancer" as mentioned is asked to a general model, not the many assistant doctor models used specifically such as scanning x-rays, MRIs, reading blood test and making the doctor's visit far faster. Look into SLMs guys. AGI or ASI has very vague definitions and its not gonna be achieved anytime soon. AND STOP USING CHATGPT.
comment_en: 1. too general. We can't just cluster all the consumer grade AI like free ChatGPT, Gemini, Tesla AutoPilot. There are small language models that we can make that run much cheaper much more reliable and "better" based on all the given definitions. 2. accountability. Depends on who the "programmer" is. If you are using free ChatGPT, YOU the consumer are the "programmer". The role PROMPT ENGINEER exists for this (it is no longer the highest demand in the space). btw Google and Claude have released manuals for this. However in the case where your Tesla AutoPilot crashes, then Tesla is responsible as you are not programming it. If you decide to run OpenPilot to run on your Corolla, then you are the programmer again.

the US doesnt want china using those powerful tech, mostly to prevent use for nuclear weapons and other military stuff using those processing capability. so what guarantee would malaysia data centers offer that would appease that worry? doesnt seem neutral if it merely helps circumvent restrictions. cauz that seems like a one sided thing isn't it? its fine staying neutral but this just seems to be cutting it close 🤔

and the argument that countries want to make sure their cloud infra is local to keep that under control, but malaysia is still a third party. will they be ok with that? if so fine, then the investments will come rolling in.

video_title: #101 | Why Is Malaysia Leading the Data Centre Race?
comment: Being a leader in the data center race looks great on paper, but the ground reality suggests it’s more of a 'forced ambition.' The foundation is clearly struggling. Just look at Johor—authorities are already slamming the brakes on new Tier 1 & 2 permits because the local power and water infrastructure simply can't handle the load. Data centers are resource-hungry monsters; a single facility can consume millions of liters of water daily, often at the expense of local citizens. The red flags are everywhere: rising utility tariffs, tightened regulations, and growing public concern over water security. It’s ironic to brag about being a global tech hub when your own people might end up paying the price through resource scarcity. True leadership is about sustainability, not just stacking concrete boxes for foreign servers.

video_title: #101 | Why Is Malaysia Leading the Data Centre Race?
comment:
My home country Ireland can provide Malaysia with a good learning and roadmap for the future. As a small country Ireland started its datacentre development journey in 2001, currently has 120 operationsl datacentres another 40 in the pipeline and the irish government has put a planning moratorium for new datacentres through 2028. Current datacentres are about 2.5gw with estimated new demand of 5.8gw. Biggest challenge is use of renewable energy. Irelands data centres uses about 35% of total power generated, which is 80% of total renewable energy. Despite massive growth of renewable energy Ireland will likeky fail.to reach its 2030 renewable  energy targets, due to datacentres. 

One thing you  mentioned is demand for water for datacentres, but this is not as a tomorrow problem, already today this is effecting the current  price of water in Johor which is effecting other high water use industries as semi con. 

Bottom line, datacentres are a great boon of investment, a major infrastructure asset for the future of Malaysia but it can be as a double edged sword. Needs very clear qnd careful planning, much as you describe.

Btw... really enjoy your videos across all topics. Thank you !  Appreciating you have directly or indirectly covered the topic before, but I believe Malaysia's biggest challenge for sustainable growth will be talent availability, both organic and external talent.
comment_en: My home country Ireland can provide Malaysia with a good learning and roadmap for the future. As a small country Ireland started its datacenter development journey in 2001, currently has 120 operational datacentres and another 40 in the pipeline and the Irish government has put a planning moratorium for new datacentres until 2028. Current datacentres are about 2.5gw with estimated new demand of 5.8gw. The biggest challenge is the use of renewable energy. Ireland's datacentres use about 35% of the total power generated, which is 80% of total renewable energy. comment:
身在其中，我的见解分享

​设备与供应链：目前数据中心的核心关键设备与零部件利润仍掌握在外国公司手中。除非马来西亚通过提高进口关税等政策手段，倒逼外国企业本地化、在马设厂制造，只靠本土的土木工程，只能赚一次性的利润。😢

​人才与长远效益：从长远来看，数据中心能为我国培训一批高端的运维与维护人才。这不仅能提升本地就业质量，更能为未来马来西亚引入和对接“核设施”等前沿科技项目做好人才技术储备。

​能源与经济变现：在数据中心建设初期，提供能源是最直接、最容易赚取的收益。当各大外国数据中心全部落成并进入稳定运营阶段后，国家可以考虑逐步调高能源价格，以此实现经济效益的最大化
comment_en: In this context, I share my views  Equipment and supply chain: the core core of the data centre's key equipment and parts profits are still in the hands of foreign companies. Except Malaysia, through policy measures such as raising import tariffs, reversing the localisation of foreign enterprises, setting up factories, and relying only on local construction, can only achieve one-off profits. 😢 Talent and long-term efficiency: From a long-term perspective, data centres can train a batch of high-end transport and maintenance talent for our country. This will not only improve the quality of employment in the country, but will also improve the future of Malaysia's introduction and access to nuclear facilities, etc.

comment:
我之前就是做Singapore data centre 的，google 在新加坡自己就有几间大的data centre ，有自己用的，也是出租给别人的。我2025 离开这行业之前听说他们的capacity 还剩很多没有用完。还有就是equinix ，很多singapore bank 都用他们的DC ，Facebook DC 从2018 到2025 都还没建完笑死我。还有新加坡自己的DC ，singtel ，ST media 等等， 给新加坡政府和商务租用。

其实Malaysia 真的赚到了，Singapore我们这里没有新的DC project，也是我离开的最主要原因，公司拿到的新project大多都是外国的DC project，需要station overseas。 可是我要讲的是，除了马来西亚，其实泰国，印尼，韩国，日本等等，还是有蛮多新DC 的计划的，不只是马来西亚在发展而已。

所以马来西亚不要以为自己就是老大，只不过是靠近新加坡还有比较稳定的地理环境，没有什么灾害，所以竞争能力好。我之前那间公司就帮aws 在泰国曼谷附近的DC ，印尼jakarta 附件也是有aws DC，韩国busan 有microsoft DC 也在广建。要真讲，鹿死谁手，还真不知道到底是哪个国家会赢，我是比较看好泰国/马来西亚。因为这几年真的发展很快，加上新加坡的经济特区的帮助，应该很大可能成为top 3 player in asia。日本韩国等等都有灾害，而且natural resource 不够马来西亚丰富。可是他们会比马来西亚更容易接触到最新的AI 晶片，还有比马来西亚更完善更强大的 IT infra。

可是现在的DC 真的也提供不了多少新的工作岗位，每次看到 google DC office 都很少人。真真是做IT 的engineer 应该都懂，要做事不一定要在office ，很多时候都是remote 的或者wfh，我自己就是每个星期差不多4天都wfh，google dc 真真需要的人才都是一些做维修的蓝领人才，而不是IT 高新人才。
comment_en: I used to be a data center owner in Singapore, Google has a few big data centers in Singapore, I use them for myself, and I rent them to others. I heard before leaving this industry in 2025 that their capacity is still a lot of unused capacity. And equinix, a lot of Singapore banks use their DC, Facebook DC from 2018 to 2025 are still not finished laughing at me. There are also big jobs in Singapore for their own DC, Singtel, STREMO, etc., to give to the government of Singapore and commercial rentals. I don't know the real Malaysia, Singapore is not a new project among us here, which is also the main reason I left, so the new project is probably a foreign one, the station needs to be completed before.

video_title: 全球科技巨头涌入大马！大马真的可以取代新加坡🇸🇬？
comment: 很多民众都觉得数据中心无法提供更多高质量就业机会。如果只是局限于而已，的确是如此。但，从整个产业链的角度就不一样了。DC需要大量的光模块做scale out 互联。光模块里的主要芯片是SiPh。很少人知道，Silterra 早在2014就开始这项研发和生产。现阶段技术也相对成熟。SiPh 全球市场也占了~15%的份额。但，大马缺了自家光模块的研发和生产线（注意，不是代工）。若有，加上政府的政策就可以形成产业链从芯片，生产到市场的闭环。打个比方，政府要求所有在大马建立的DC 得使用10%大马研发和生产的光模块。初期有了稳定的内需，加上Silterra SiPh 芯片，就可以开始往外扩不再局限于内需。所以，政府的政策非常重要。先招外资入场创造需求，整合产业链，加以政策。让本地产业链有baseline market, 再外扩海外市场。第一步已经到位，接下来就看政府和半导体相关公司的合作和布局了。
comment_en: Many people feel that data centers can not provide more high-quality jobs. If only limited to them, it is indeed so. But, from the point of view of the entire industrial chain, it is different. DC needs a large number of optical modules to scale out
clean_comment: many people feel data center provide high quality job limited indeed point view entire industrial chain different dc need large number optical module scale

comment:
​ @123wkk123 数据中心只是AI运算（机械，电脑），不需要现场人工管理。你若要在美国远程控制（监管）是没问题。

噪音不是来自电脑设备，是冷气散热器材。AI 镜片越细（nano），都会有散热问题。你不信，去看美国人如何投诉。连微软（Microsoft）都把附近的住宅区买下后，不让任何人居住。

数据中心是AI自动运算的（像Google Gemini， 只是个软体），只需要保安人员看管，他们找外劳保安在外边站守而已。他不是IT 办公室，不会有电脑技术人员长期监管。

万一电脑故障并不会直接瘫痪系统，系统一般会有mirroring system，harddisk 坏，还有另一组HD可以用。主机坏，还有副机会起动。远程监控的工作人员会看到机械故障的显示，就会叫外包技术人员去维修硬体。一般维修不需要关闭系统，除非特别事件需要reboot， 用户是察觉不到的。我以前的工作就是维修这些server，storage 的。
comment_en:
Data centers are just for AI computing (machines, computers), no on-site human management is needed. If you want to remotely control (supervise) it from the US, that's totally fine.  

The noise doesn't come from the computer equipment, it's from the air conditioners and cooling equipment. The finer the AI chips (nano), the more heat they generate. If you don't believe it, just see how Americans complain about it. Even Microsoft bought nearby residential areas so that no one could live nearby.  

Data centers run AI computations automatically (like Google Gemini, which is just software), they only need security staff to watch over them. They hire outsourced security guards to stand outside. It's not an IT office, there won't be computer techs constantly supervising.  

If the computers fail, it won't crash the system immediately because systems usually have mirroring. If a hard disk fails, there's another one to use. If the main machine fails, a backup will start up. Remote monitoring staff will see mechanical failure alerts and call outsourced techs to fix the hardware. Most repairs don't require shutting down the system, unless in special cases that need a reboot. Users wouldn't notice. I used to work maintaining these servers and storages.

我之前就是做Singapore data centre 的，google 在新加坡自己就有几间大的data centre ，有自己用的，也是出租给别人的。我2025 离开这行业之前听说他们的capacity 还剩很多没有用完。还有就是equinix ，很多singapore bank 都用他们的DC ，Facebook DC 从2018 到2025 都还没建完笑死我。还有新加坡自己的DC ，singtel ，ST media 等等， 给新加坡政府和商务租用。

其实Malaysia 真的赚到了，Singapore我们这里没有新的DC project，也是我离开的最主要原因，公司拿到的新project大多都是外国的DC project，需要station overseas。 可是我要讲的是，除了马来西亚，其实泰国，印尼，韩国，日本等等，还是有蛮多新DC 的计划的，不只是马来西亚在发展而已。

所以马来西亚不要以为自己就是老大，只不过是靠近新加坡还有比较稳定的地理环境，没有什么灾害，所以竞争能力好。我之前那间公司就帮aws 在泰国曼谷附近的DC ，印尼jakarta 附件也是有aws DC，韩国busan 有microsoft DC 也在广建。要真讲，鹿死谁手，还真不知道到底是哪个国家会赢，我是比较看好泰国/马来西亚。因为这几年真的发展很快，加上新加坡的经济特区的帮助，应该很大可能成为top 3 player in asia。日本韩国等等都有灾害，而且natural resource 不够马来西亚丰富。可是他们会比马来西亚更容易接触到最新的AI 晶片，还有比马来西亚更完善更强大的 IT infra。

comment:
@gohyonghongluke3443 
放弃大马公民其实有几个层面。很多柔佛州的人（包括我自己在内），因为家庭成员一半以上都在新加坡。

有些人是因为在新加坡的工作受到重用，拿了PR一段时间后，政府就会要求他们做出选择。也有人是夫妻一方成为新加坡公民，另一方只拿PR，他们的计划是：在新加坡赚钱，等老了再回马来西亚养老。

所以，不要只看表面的数字。其实很多人改天在新加坡赚够钱，还是会回来马来西亚，或者申请第二家园。这背后都有周全的考量。最主要的原因还是现实问题：新币比马币强太多。

潘建成选择回来，把这样的发展带动起来，其实是很有前景的。希望我们马来西亚能够好好珍惜，从现在这个层面去着手。就好像40年前，台湾也有了台积电一样。
comment_en:
Giving up Malaysian citizenship actually has several layers. Many people from Johor (including myself) have more than half of their family members living in Singapore.  

Some people do it because their jobs in Singapore are highly valued, and after holding PR for a while, the government requires them to make a choice. There are also couples where one becomes a Singaporean citizen while the other only has PR; their plan is to earn money in Singapore and then return to Malaysia to retire.  

So, don’t just look at the surface numbers. In fact, many people will come back to Malaysia or apply for a second home once they’ve earned enough money in Singapore. There’s careful consideration behind all of this. The main reason is practical: the Singapore dollar is much stronger than the Malaysian ringgit.  

Pan Jiancheng chose to come back and drive this kind of development, which is actually very promising. Hopefully, we in Malaysia can truly value this and start working from this level. It’s just like 40 years ago when Taiwan had TSMC.

comment_en: Only Sohai believes that the presence of AI data centers in Malaysia is a good thing for Malaysia. First, AI data centers are just databases, not intensive industries. Second, the location requirements for these AI databases are simply cheap pure water and electricity. In other words, it competes with humans for water and electricity. The reason Johor has experienced strange water outages during floods in recent years is because prioritizing the supply of clean water to data centers comes at the expense of residents' water needs. Only during droughts do you realize how cruel and unreasonable this is. Third, these AI centers like Google and Microsoft don't get a share of Malaysia's profits; they only pay utilities and land rentals. Does Malaysia's talent resources really get you high positions or improve the environment? None of it, just making wedding clothes for people! In fact, we don't need so much AI, or rather, AI shouldn't be deployed in places like ours, where people are large and resources are scarce. It should be built in places like Russia and China, which have vast land, sparse populations, water resources, and cheap energy. This is the best way to meet the environment and cost. But Westerners, for their own hegemony, are unwilling to cooperate with these so-called hostile countries and want cheap construction on their own land. The U.S. itself has plenty of resources suitable for this, but these nasty capitalists sacrifice developing countries' resources to maximize profits for them.
